query: How do I edit the kernel command line in RHEL 9 and RHEL 10?
contexts: Root Cause Encountered while performing an in place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 using Leapp XFS V4 was deprecated in RHEL 9 and support for it has been removed in RHEL 10 XFS V5 has been the default since RHEL 7.3 you may encounter this issue on systems that have been updated from an older version. Diagnostic Steps RHEL 9 prints the following warning to the journal when mounting an XFS V4 file system: Jun 16 12:57:03 rhel9 kernel: XFS (vdb): Deprecated V4 format (crc=0) will not be supported beginning with RHEL10 Jun 16 12:57:03 rhel9 kernel: XFS (vdb): Mounting V4 Filesystem Leapp pre upgrade check shows the following warning Upgrade has been inhibited due to the following problems: 1. Detected XFS filesystems incompatible with target kernel. Check the version of XFS file systems with the following bash command. The output will say okay or not okay. In this example /dev/vdb has an XFS V4 file system and /dev/vdc has an XFS V5 system. mount | grep "type xfs" | awk '{print $1}' | while read line ; do echo "$line: $(xfs_info $line | grep crc | awk '{if ($2=="crc=1") {print "okay"} else {print "not okay"}}')" done /dev/vdb: not okay /dev/vdc: okay mkfs.xfs on RHEL 9 prints a warning that XFS V4 file systems are deprecated # mkfs.xfs -m crc=0 -n ftype=0 /dev/vdb V4 filesystems are deprecated and will not be supported by future versions.

Where is the sgdisk command in RHEL 10? Solution Verified - Updated 20 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 GPT (GUID Partition Table) Issue Unable to locate sgdisk in RHEL 10 It seems like gdisk module has been removed from the official repositories in RHEL 10. It's currently available in RHEL 9 and earlier releases, has it been deprecated in RHEL 10 or is there an alternate method of installing it? Resolution Install the parted package. yum install parted Usage is covered in the product documentation: RHEL 10 - Managing File Systems - Partition operations with parted Workaround - Unsupported EPEL Package The gdisk package is in EPEL: How to use Extra Packages for Enterprise Linux (EPEL)? Root Cause The gdisk package was marked as deprecated in RHEL 9.6 , and is removed from RHEL 10 .

How to modify the kernel command-line in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and 10 Solution Verified - Updated 30 Jul 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 10 grub2 Issue How to modify the boot parameter in RHEL 9 and RHEL 10 grub2-editenv - list command output is not showing kernelopts option to update kernel commandline. # grub2-editenv - listsaved_entry=d7a9453a54b34038ab12deddd44484e6-5.14.0-39.el9.aarch64boot_success=0 Resolution Solution 1 - Update the kernel command-line via grubby Verify the current arguments # grubby --info DEFAULTindex=0kernel="/boot/vmlinuz-5.14.0-39.el9.aarch64"args="ro crashkernel=2G-:448M rd.lvm.lv=rhel/root rd.lvm.lv=rhel/swap"root="/dev/mapper/rhel-root"initrd="/boot/initramfs-5.14.0-39.el9.aarch64.img"title="Red Hat Enterprise Linux (5.14.0-39.el9.aarch64) 9.0 (Plow)"id="d7a9453a54b34038ab12deddd44484e6-5.14.0-39.el9.aarch64" Add the command-line parameters with option to update the current kernel or all kernel # grubby --update-kernel=/boot/vmlinuz-$(uname -r) --args="<NEW_PARAMETER>" or # grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args="<NEW_PARAMETER>" or # grubby --args <NEW_PARAMETER> --update-kernel DEFAULT Similarly we may remove any kernel command-line parameters with below option # grubby --update-kernel=ALL --remove-args="<PARAMETER_TO_REMOVE>" Reboot the system for changes to reflect # systemctl reboot Solution 2 - Edit /etc/default/grub and rebuild the menu Edit /etc/default/grub file and update the kernel c [...]

RHEL-9/RHEL-10: Kernel panic during cached_dir_offload_close Solution Unverified - Updated 23 Feb 2026 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Seen on 5.14.0-503.35.1.el9_5 / kernel-5.14.0-570.41.1.el9_6 / kernel-5.14.0-570.60.1.el9_6 cifs Issue Observed behaviors include servers becoming unresponsive,unexpected system reboots. This occurs because a cached directory handle is incorrectly reused after being freed. Resolution Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.1.z The issue has been resolved with kernel-6.12.0-124.29.1.el10_1 via Errata RHSA-2026:1178 . The issue was tracked at private JIRA RHEL-128581. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0.z The issue is being tracked at private JIRA RHEL-128580. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.7.z The issue has been resolved with kernel-5.14.0-611.24.1.el9_7 via Errata RHSA-2026:0793 . The issue was tracked at private JIRA RHEL-127422. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.6.z The issue has been resolved with kernel-5.14.0-570.79.1.el9_6 via Errata RHSA-2026:0804 . The issue was tracked at private JIRA RHEL-127421. Workaround It is possible to use the nohandlecache option when mounting via CIFS, although this may impact performance. Root Cause The symptom implies a use-after-free issue caused by a race condition. The upstream commits will resolve the issue: 734e99623c5b ("smb: client: fix potential UAF in smb2_close_cached_fid()")5c76f9961c17 ("smb: client: fix potential cfid UAF in smb2_query_info_compound") Diagnostic Steps vmcore analysis: KER [...]

How do I permanently modify the kernel command line on RHEL 7? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 7 GRUB2 boot loader Issue How do I modify the kernel command line (and, thus, kernel boot parameters) in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 with GRUB2 installed as a boot loader? Resolution For RHEL7: Alternative 1: Make copy of the GRUB2 configuration files as a precaution: For a BIOS-based system: # cp /etc/default/grub /etc/default/grub-backup # cp /boot/grub2/grub.cfg /boot/grub2/grub.cfg-backup For a UEFI-based system: # cp /etc/default/grub /etc/default/grub-backup# cp /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg-backup Open the /etc/default/grub file for editing: # vi /etc/default/grub This file contains multiple GRUB2 options. Kernel boot parameters are specified by the GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX option. Once the file is edited, save it and run the command specified below to generate a new grub.cfg file: For a BIOS-based system: # grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg For a UEFI-based system: # grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg Finally, reboot the system to apply the changes: # systemctl reboot Alternative 2: Using grubby command: For checking details of default kernel: # grubby --info DEFAULT For adding parameters on all kernels: # grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args="argA argB" For removing parameters on all kernels: # grubby --update-kernel=ALL --remove-args="argA argB" Reboot the system to apply the changes:  [...]

How do I permanently modify the kernel command line in RHEL 8? Solution Verified - Updated 20 Jun 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 GRUB2 boot loader Issue The boot loader Grand Unified Boot Loader (GRUB2) in RHEL 8 differs from the GRUB2 in RHEL 7. How do I modify the kernel command line (and, thus, kernel boot parameters) in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? Resolution Method 1: Using grub2-editenv grub2-editenv command can be used to update the /boot/grub2/grubenv file: RHEL 8 : List the kernel parameters from the kernelopts variable: # grub2-editenv - list | grep kernelopts The following commands can be used to add/remove/modify the kernel command line parameters: # grub2-editenv - set "$(grub2-editenv - list | grep kernelopts) <NEW_PARAMETER>" OR # grub2-editenv - set "kernelopts=root=/dev/mapper/rhel-root ro crashkernel=auto resume=/dev/mapper/rhel-swap rd.lvm.lv=rhel/root rd.lvm.lv=rhel/swap rhgb quiet" Verify the newly added output: # grub2-editenv - list | grep kernelopts Reboot the system to apply the changes: # systemctl reboot Note: If you have to abort the changes then you can unset the kernelopts with the following command: # grub2-editenv - unset kernelopts Note: The older method of modification of kernel command line from file /etc/default/grub followed by the rebuilding of grub ( grub2-mkconfig ) is still possible. However, it is recommended to use the ways mentioned in this solution for modifying the kernel command line on RHEL 8. Method 2: Using grubby  [...]

What is the difference between "module_blacklist" & "modprobe.blacklist" kernel command line in RHEL? Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Which kernel command line parameter to use when blacklisting the module? Why do two kernel command line parameters exist for the same purpose? Resolution The modprobe.blacklist will prevent the automatic loading of the module by kernel however if any user/app tries to load it manually or if it is a dependency of any module that is being loaded either automatically or manually for example using insmod then this module will also get loaded whereas the module_blacklist kernel parameter will freeze this module load irrespective of any condition introduced with the following patch: modules: Add kernel parameter to blacklist modules Blacklisting a module in linux has long been a problem. The current procedure is to use rd.blacklist=module_name, however, that doesn't cover the case after the initramfs and before a boot prompt (where one is supposed to use /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist.conf to blacklist runtime loading). Using rd.shell to get an early prompt is hit-or-miss, and doesn't cover all situations AFAICT. This patch adds this functionality of permanently blacklisting a module by its name via the kernel parameter module_blacklist=module_name. Diagnostic Steps Modify the kernel command line to include modprobe.blacklist=<module_name> or module_ [...]

How to edit bond connections configured via kernel command line for RHCOS nodes? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Dec 2024 Environment Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform Red Hat CoreOS Issue One of the physical interfaces of a LACP bonding gets renamed after upgrade from OCP 4.12 to 4.13 (Using Mellanox ConnectX-5 NICs) Network card with two interfaces, one interface had its name changed from eno34 to eno34np1 . During upgrade from RHEL 8 to RHEL 9 (4.12 --> 4.13 OCP) Resolution Occasionally, an incorrect or outdated bonding configuration in grub.cfg can cause RCHOS node to not come with functional network . The following next steps show how to manually modify the boot parameter to fix the problem and let the system boot successfully with networking up. When the list of kernels appears at the beginning of the boot process, select the kernel to boot using the up/down arrow keys. Press key e to edit this entry. Use the arrow keys to move to the linux line. Append or change any necessary kernel options.Mention the right nic names , example (enp34s0f0np0,enp34s0f1np1) Press Ctrl-x to boot the system with the modified kernel options. If the system boots properly, the changes will need to be saved in the grub configuration file. Execute the below command to modify the kernel command line permanently.

query: How do I change the EFI boot order in Red Hat Enterprise Linux?
contexts: Testing JDK IBM JDK Eclipse OpenJ9 Operating systems Although support for these platforms was deprecated in a previous JBoss EAP release, some artifacts and resources linked to these platforms were not removed, such as the wildfly-openssl native library binding . For Red Hat JBoss Enterprise Application Platform 7.4, those artifacts and resources have been removed. Changes between Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 and 7 Updated 14 Sept 2016 System and command changes between RHEL 6 and RHEL 7 Between RHEL6 and RHEL7 there are a number of changes to tools, commands, and workflows. Changes that are likely to affect common administrative tasks are listed here: Anaconda RHEL installer completely redesigned 1 Legacy GRUB boot loader replaced by GRUB2 2 Procedure for bypassing root password prompt at boot completely different 3 SysV init system and all related tools replaced by systemd 4 ext4 replaced by xfs as default filesystem type 5 Directories /bin , /sbin , /lib and /lib64 are now all under the /usr directory 6 Network interfaces have a new naming scheme based on physical device location (e.g., eth0 might become enp0s3 ) 7 ntpd replaced by chronyd as the default network time protocol daemon 8 GNOME2 replaced by GNOME3 as default desktop environment 9 System registration and subscription now handled exclusively with Red Hat Subscription Management (RHSM) 10 MySQL replaced by Mariadb 11 tgtd replaced by targetcli 12 High Availability Add-On: RGManager removed as resource-management option (in favor of Pacemaker); all CMAN features merged into Corosync ( qdiskd replaced by votequorum plugin); all tools unified into pcs 13 ifconfig and route commands are further deprecated in favor of ip netstat further deprecated in favor of ss System user UID range extended from 0-499 to 0-999 locate no longer available by default; (available as mlocate package) [...]

RHEL7 EFI boot order variables being replicated Updated 22 Jan 2015 Issue When booting \efi\BOOT\BOOTX64.efi, a new boot order variable is being created causing the BootOrder list to be duplicated. The previous BootOrder list is replicated with the new boot order variable added to the middle of the new BootOrder list. This causes the new boot order variable to be added to a spot in the BootOrder list such that it won't be chosen by default. Environment RHEL7EFI boot Root Cause This is a known bug, and has been described and patched in the following upstream GitHub discussion: Content from github.com is not included. GitHub Summary:When booting \efi\BOOT\BOOTX64.efi, a new boot order variable is being created. The previous BootOrder list is replicated with the new boot order variable added to the middle of the new BootOrder list. The new boot order variable is added to a spot in the BootOrder list such that it won't be chosen by default.

Does "/usr/bin/raw" command available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9? Solution Verified - Updated 3 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 kernel-5.14.0-70.22.1.el9_0.x86_64 and later util-linux-2.37.4-3.el9.x86_64 and later Issue Which package provides the /usr/bin/raw command on RHEL 9? Can't find /usr/bin/raw command on RHEL9?

Why does UEFI PXE boot of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.1 fail? Solution Unverified - Updated 17 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.1 UEFI PXE Issue Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.1 machine is configured to work as a UEFI PXE Server. The distributions 7.0 and 7.2 work as expected as they are hosted on the UEFI PXE Server, whereas 7.1 UEFI PXE boot fails. Resolution Use the latest BOOTX64.EFI file from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 instead of using the one from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.1 Root Cause The BOOTX64.EFI from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.1 seem to have problem. Even changelog shows no difference, still the BOOTX64.EFI seems to be different. [root@rhel7 ~]# rpm -qp /mnt/Packages/shim-0.9-2.el7.x86_64.rpm --changelog > /tmp/shim-0.7-8.el7_0.x86_64.txt[root@rhel7 ~]# rpm -qp /mnt/Packages/shim-0.7-8.el7_0.x86_64.rpm --changelog > /tmp/shim-0.7-8.el7_0.x86_64.txt[root@rhel7 ~]# diff /tmp/shim-0.7-8.el7_0.x86_64.txt /tmp/shim-0.7-8.el7_0.x86_64.txtBut, the files BOOTX64.EFI are actually different from Each Others.

Red Hat Enterprise Linux Installation cannot proceed because of missing UEFI partition Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Observed in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, but the problem is expected to appear in other versions as well. Issue Unable to proceed with the Build process because of unknown disk format. Failed to find a suitable stage1 device efi system partition cannot be of type xfs. When selecting Automatic partitioning, no issues occurred, but when custom partitions are configured the installation cannot proceed. Resolution There are two ways to address this issue: Reconfigure partitioning to add an EFI partition and make sure it is configured to be mounted on /boot/efi . Change the server boot configuration to boot from legacy BIOS instead of UEFI. This is a change that needs to be done in the server FW, outside of the OS installation. Root Cause A UEFI system needs a dedicated EFI partition for booting, according to the UEFI specification. Diagnostic Steps When completing the "Manual Partitioning" step in the installer GUI, note the yellow error line at the bottom with a message similar to: Error checking storage configuration. Click for details or press Done again to continue. Clicking for details (as the message suggests) prints the message: Failed to find a suitable stage1 device: EFI System Partition cannot be of type xfs.; EFI System Partition must be mounted on one of /boot/efi.; EFI System Partition cannot be of type lvmpv. In the installer use  [...]

After applying system updates, rebooting system stuck at the EFI boot menu loader. Solution Verified - Updated 18 Aug 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 UEFI Issue System reboot stuck at EFI boot menu loader after applying system updates. Error messages as below: Failed to open \efi\boot\grubx64.efi - Not FoundFailed to load image ??: Not Foundstart_image() returned Not Found, falling back to default loaderFailed to open \efi\boot\grubx64.efi - Not FoundFailed to load image ??: Not Foundstart_image() returned Not Found System boots normal before applying the updates. Resolution Rebuild the menu -  How to boot a system into rescue mode after booting on the DVD in 'Troubleshooting' mode and entering the chroot : # grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg Then open the file /boot/grub2/grub.cfg and confirm it shows set root='hd0,gptX' . Root Cause This is because system is UEFI, it has a GPT table: $ cat sos_commands/block/fdisk_-l_.dev.vda Disk /dev/vda: 30 GiB, 32212254720 bytes, 62914560 sectorsUnits: sectors of 1 * 512 = 512 bytesSector size (logical/physical): 512 bytes / 512 bytesI/O size (minimum/optimal): 512 bytes / 512 bytesDisklabel type: gpt <----[...] In the problematic system, initially grub.cfg sets the "root" to "(hd0,msdos1)", as found in /boot/grub2/grub.cfg : [...]### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###insmod part_msdosinsmod ext2set root='hd0,msdos1' <---[...] It is expected to see: `set root='hd0,gptX'` Diagnostic Steps Confirm the boot order for your syst [...]

Could not change permission for files under /boot/efi Solution Verified - Updated 9 Feb 2026 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue Could not change permission for /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.conf on RHEL 6 : [root@rhel6 redhat]# ls -ltr total 248 -rwx------ 1 root root 247570 Jan 22 2011 grub.efi -rwx------ 1 root root 887 May 20 2014 grub.conf [root@rhel6 redhat]# pwd /boot/efi/EFI/redhat [root@rhel6 redhat]# chmod 600 grub.conf [root@rhel6 redhat]# ls -ltr total 248 -rwx------ 1 root root 247570 Jan 22 2011 grub.efi -rwx------ 1 root root 887 May 20 2014 grub.conf Could not change permission for /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg on RHEL 7 and above: [root@rhel8 ~]# cd /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/ [root@rhel8 redhat]# ls -ltr total 4884 -rwx------. 1 root root 2285512 Feb 25 2021 grubx64.efi drwx------. 2 root root 4096 Feb 25 2021 fonts -rwx------. 1 root root 918944 Apr 7 2021 shimx64-redhat.efi -rwx------. 1 root root 924888 Apr 7 2021 shimx64.efi -rwx------. 1 root root 846856 Apr 7 2021 mmx64.efi -rwx------. 1 root root 182 Apr 7 2021 BOOTX64.CSV -rwx------. 1 root root 1024 Jan 26 11:33 grubenv -rwx------. 1 root root 6374 Jan 26 11:33 grub.cfg [root@rhel8 redhat]# pwd /boot/efi/EFI/redhat [root@rhel8 redhat]# chmod 600 grub.cfg [root@rhel8 redhat]# ls -ltr total 4884 -rwx------. 1 root root 2285512 Feb 25 2021 grubx64.efi drwx------. 2 root root 4096 Feb 25 2021 font [...]

How to remove the grub password in Red Hat Enterprise Linux? Solution Verified - Updated 16 Sept 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue How to remove the grub password protection when the password is lost? Resolution RHEL 6 and below versions When the system is online. The password hash is generated by grub-crypt tool and this hash is used in the /boot/grub/grub.conf file to protect the grub. default=0 timeout=5 splashimage=(hd0,0)/grub/splash.xpm.gz password --encrypted $6$uXBKuGoJQOQO3pfR$hSsWyDSnJK37/Ro117aa0488VyRSK9OKE5mpeeYDSgVnceG3M6W6ROencghVeZtq7qPwwn1zOUlEI6tRaKuHg0title Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Take backup of the /boot/grub/grub.conf file. # cp /boot/grub/grub.conf /boot/grub/grub.conf-backup Remove or comment out the password --encrypted HASH line from the /boot/grub/grub.conf file. #password --encrypted $6$uXBKuGoJQOQO3pfR$hSsWyDSnJK37/Ro117aa0488VyRSK9OKE5mpeeYDSgVnceG3M6W6ROencghVeZtq7qPwwn1zOUlEI6tRaKuHg0title Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 When the system is down. Boot the system in the rescue mode. Take backup of the /boot/grub/grub.conf file. # cp /boot/grub/grub.conf /boot/grub/grub.conf-backup Remove or comment out the password --encrypted HASH line from the /boot/grub/grub.conf file. #password --encrypted $6$uXBKuGoJQOQO3pfR$hSsWyDSnJK37/Ro117aa0488VyRSK9OKE5mpeeYDSgVnceG3M6W6ROencghVeZtq7qPwwn1zOUlEI6tRaKuHg0title Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 RHEL 7 and above version [...]

Failed to install Red Hat Enterprise Linux on IBM X3650 M3 Server with UEFI enabled Solution Verified - Updated 6 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 IBM x3650 M3 Issue After having the disk partitioned by Anaconda and clicking the 'Next' button the following error occurs: "You have not created a /boot/efi partition" Resolution Switch to "Legacy Only" option in the IBM UEFI menu. This option must be put at the top of the boot sequence. If you insert the "Legacy Only" flag into the boot order list, the server invokes the compatibility module for booting regardless of whether there appears to be anything there to boot (that is, UEFI is not aware of media behind non-UEFI compliant adapters). When the Legacy Only option is invoked, the server is committed to a BIOS boot, and the server eventually resets if nothing is booted during the BIOS boot process. Root Cause Anaconda FAILED to find the /boot/efi partition and raises this error.

Understanding the part of the GRUB Bootloader in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 and 6 Updated 13 Jun 2014 Introduction All information and references to GRUB are dealing with GNU GRUB version 1( GRUB Legacy ) in this article This article includes information specific to interaction between GRUB and MBR(Master Boot Record)-based storage devices in a Red Hat Enterprise Linux environment, there are other types of bootloaders, boot managers, and partitioning schemes not mentioned due to scope of this article It is important to understand the role of the bootloader when learning about the boot process of a Linux system.

Does "/usr/bin/raw" command available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9? Solution Verified - Updated 3 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 kernel-5.14.0-70.22.1.el9_0.x86_64 and later util-linux-2.37.4-3.el9.x86_64 and later Issue Which package provides the /usr/bin/raw command on RHEL 9? Can't find /usr/bin/raw command on RHEL9?

How to sign the grub bootloader with my own keys? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment UEFI Secure Boot Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Issue How to sign the grub bootloader with my own keys? How to generate keys for Secure Boot without trusting Microsoft or RHEL distribution keys? Resolution You have to install the pesign package coming with the Optional repositories (e.g. rhel-7-server-optional-rpms ) # yum install pesign This package comes with two binaries:- pesign , which is used to sign your EFI images.- efikeygen , which can generate standard X509 certs, stored in a BDB database.

Getting Grub prompt after rebooting the system with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 installation Solution Verified - Updated 28 May 2012 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Issue I installed Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 with interactive GUI mode. But, I got grub prompt after reboot. It seems that the grub was not loaded to proper device on installation. Resolution Select the option "The Grub Bootloader will be installed on your-device " during RHEL installation. replace your-device by the preferable boot device like /dev/sda, /dev/hda, and /dev/cciss/c0d0. Move the boot device into the top of drive order list at the boot loader advanced configuration screen. To configure boot loader manually with auto-partitioning, check "Review and modify partitioning layout". Otherwise the bootloader configuration screen will be skipped. Root Cause Boot loader was not installed because selected the option "No boot loader will be installed" during installation. Or, the boot device was not specified at the top of driver order list at the boot loader advanced configuration screen.

Red Hat Enterprise Linux Rescue Options Solution Verified - Updated 7 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 4 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Issue System is unable to boot or filesystem is not accessible. Resolution Single User Mode Booting into single user mode using GRUB is accomplished by editing the kernel line. This assumes that either the GRUB boot menu is not password protected or that you have access to the password if it is.

OpenSCAP scans are failing on grub bootloader password check Solution Verified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Issue OpenSCAP shows fail result on the xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_bootloader_password rule Grub is password protected but does not pass on the OpenSCAP scans when using the stig-rhel7-disa profile stig-rhel7-disa profile scans are failing on grub bootloader after using grub2-setpassword to set the password OpenSCAP scan is failing on CCE-27309-4 Resolution Run the following command to set a password for grub, this will create the /boot/grub2/user.cfg file # grub2-setpasswordEnter password: Confirm password: The following command will edit the /etc/grub.d/01_users file and replace the \${GRUB2_PASSWORD} with your password from /boot/grub2/user.cfg . You will need to change grubuser to whatever username you would like to use to login with on the grub menu. In order to pass the check for CCE-27309-4 How to password-protect the GRUB2 boot menu in RHEL 7 states the grub2-setpassword utility only supports configurations where there is a single root user, however I have confirmed that it's possible to run the utility, and combine the output with the old way of configuring a grub password before RHEL 7.2. This allows the system to utilize the newer way while still passing the OpenSCAP checks as a workaround.

Can XFS be used for /boot and / as a filesystem in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6? Solution Unverified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Red Hat Scalable File System Issue Can XFS be used for /boot and / as a filesystem in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6? Resolution XFS can not be used for /boot and / as a filesystem in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6. If XFS is selected for / or /boot at install time, Anaconda will flag it as an error, and disallow the configuration. The XFS filesystem can be used for creating larger filesystems, but one can not use XFS for /boot and / partitioning. The GRUB bootloader in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 supports only the ext2, ext3 , and ext4 (recommended) filesystems. Any other filesystem for /boot can not be used, such as Btrfs , XFS , or VFAT . See the "GRUB Boot Loader" section in the Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Installation Guide . The plan is to make XFS the default File System for / root volume in RHEL 7. Important Points: The XFS kernel module/driver is included in the standard RHEL kernel.

System prints "GRUB" during boot and shows no menu on Red Hat Enterprise Linux Solution Verified - Updated 7 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 3 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 4 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 GRUB bootloader Issue System prints "GRUB" on boot and does not proceed to boot menu. Resolution Boot the system from the binary DVD or boot disc1 of the same major release as the system. Once the system has successfully booted from the ISO image and Red Hat Enterprise Linux boot screen will appear. Type: "linux rescue" without the quotes, and hit enter at the prompt. [F1-Main] [F2-Options] [F3-General] [F4-Kernel] [F5-Rescue] boot: linux rescue When prompted for language, and keyboard, provide the pertinent information for the system. When prompted to enable the network devices on the system, select: No Select: Continue when prompted to allow the rescue environment to mount Red Hat Enterprise Linux installation under /mnt/sysimage directory.

System doesn't boot after installation of Red Hat Enterprise Linux. Solution Verified - Updated 6 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (any) Issue After installation of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.3 on newly purchased hardware, system doesn't boot. Resolution Install the grub bootloader through rescue mode. Check the Solution 1521 for more details. Root Cause When anaconda installer installs Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.3 (or any other version/release of RHEL), at that time, it creates a kickstart file anaconda-ks.cfg in root directory which contains all the information which was asked to user for installation. Checked the file /root/anaconda-ks.cfg , and it was seen that the bootloader was installed on wrong device. $ grep bootloader root/anaconda-ks.cfg bootloader --location=partition --driveorder=sda --append="crashkernel=auto rhgb quiet" The bootloader shall be installed on MBR , and when one install it on MBR , the following entry appears in the /root/anaconda-ks.cfg file. # grep bootloader /root/anaconda-ks.cfgbootloader --location=mbr --driveorder=vda --append="crashkernel=auto rhgb quiet" So the RCA of this issue was "Bootloader was not installed on MBR during installation"
